---
name: frontend-designer
description: Master frontend designer with deep expertise in color theory, visual hierarchy, design patterns, and composition. Use this skill when creating visually striking, user-centric interfaces. Creates eye-catching designs with strategic color palettes, optimal element placement, and cohesive visual systems that guide user attention and emotion.
license: MIT
---

This skill guides the creation of exceptional frontend designs using color theory, visual hierarchy, spatial composition, and psychological principles to create memorable, effective interfaces.

## Core Design Mastery

**Color Theory Expertise**: Choose base colors with purpose—consider brand identity, target audience psychology, and competitor differentiation. Build cohesive palettes using 60-30-10 rule: 60% dominant color, 30% secondary, 10% accent. Understand color psychology (blue=trust, red=urgency, green=growth, purple=luxury) and apply contextually. Create depth with tints, shades, and tones. Ensure accessibility with proper contrast ratios (4.5:1 minimum for text).

**Visual Hierarchy Mastery**: Guide user attention through strategic size, contrast, color, spacing, and positioning. Most important elements get largest size, highest contrast, or prime position (top-left for F-pattern reading). Use white space generously to create breathing room and emphasis. Apply the rule of thirds for balanced composition. Create clear visual paths using directional cues, alignment, and proximity.

**Strategic Element Placement**: Position call-to-action buttons in natural eye flow paths (right side for forward actions, center for primary actions). Place navigation where users expect (top or left). Hero sections above fold with compelling visuals. Use Z-pattern for landing pages, F-pattern for text-heavy content. Balance asymmetry with visual weight distribution.

**Design System Consistency**: Establish cohesive spacing scale (multiples of 4 or 8), consistent border radius values, unified shadow system for depth perception, and typographic hierarchy with clear size relationships. Every element should feel part of the same family.

**Emotional Design**: Create mood through color temperature (warm=energetic/friendly, cool=calm/professional), evoke feelings with intentional palette choices, use motion to delight and guide, and apply typography personality (serif=traditional/trustworthy, sans-serif=modern/clean).

Remember: Great design is invisible. Users should feel guided, not confused. Every color, placement, and visual choice should serve the user's goal.
